food_name: Sayur Lodeh (mixed vegetable in coconut milk curry)
region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia — widely adopted in Singapore Malay cuisine
food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable and tofu in light coconut milk broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sayur Lodeh. Closest parent is mixed vegetable coconut milk curry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed vegetables in light coconut milk broth with lemongrass and turmeric (Javanese-Malay sayur lodeh)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Sayur Lodeh serving is approximately 300g: 150g mixed vegetables and tofu puffs in 150g coconut milk broth. Served alongside lontong rice cake or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Sayur Lodeh — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Table
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo has a direct Sayur Lodeh entry. Values per 100g represent the mixed vegetables + tofu puffs + coconut milk broth composite. Frequently served with lontong as the curry component — noted in meal context.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — Vegetable-dominant dish with very low carbohydrate (5.5g/100g). GI driven by minor carbohydrate from vegetables (long beans, carrot, cabbage — all low-GI). Coconut milk fat further suppresses glycaemic response. Estimated composite GI ~35–42. Per-300g bowl delivers approximately 16.5g carbohydrate; GL per bowl ~6.3 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sayur Lodeh has one of the lower sodium profiles among Malay curries (195mg/100g). A 300g bowl delivers approximately 585mg sodium. The main sodium source is the spice paste (shrimp paste if used). Many versions are prepared without belacan — check preparation.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension can freely enjoy sayur lodeh — it is one of the lowest-sodium Malay curry preparations. Request no belacan or reduced belacan to minimise sodium further. This is one of the best Malay curry choices for hypertension patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sayur Lodeh is one of the best Malay curry choices for patients with type 2 diabetes — very low GI (~38), very low GL per bowl (~6.3), and high dietary fibre (1.8g/100g) from mixed vegetables. The vegetable-dominant composition with minimal starch is ideal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should actively choose sayur lodeh at lontong or nasi padang stalls. It is the lowest-GL curry side dish in Malay cuisine. The tofu puffs provide protein without adding carbohydrate. Eating sayur lodeh alongside higher-GL foods moderates the overall meal glycaemic response.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk saturated fat (4.5g/100g) is the primary concern. However, the high vitamin C (12mg/100g) from cabbage and vegetables — a 300g bowl provides 36mg (40% of the 90mg/day RDA) — and folate (22mcg/100g) are beneficial for joint health and anti-inflammatory response.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sayur Lodeh is among the best Malay curry choices for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The high vegetable content provides vitamin C and folate that support joint health. The turmeric in the base provides curcumin. Limit to 2–3 times per week due to saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sayur Lodeh is a light, mildly spiced mixed vegetable curry in coconut milk that is the vegetable companion to lontong — it is almost always served together with the compressed rice cake. Long beans, carrot, cabbage, eggplant, and tofu puffs are simmered in a golden, fragrant broth of diluted coconut milk seasoned with lemongrass, galangal, candlenuts, and turmeric. Unlike the thick, concentrated coconut curries of rendang or opor, sayur lodeh uses a lighter coconut milk broth — the vegetables are simmered until tender but retain their texture. It is one of the most nutritionally balanced Malay dishes: low calorie, low GI, high fibre, and with meaningful vitamin C from the vegetables.

confidence_reason: Direct entry in HPB Singapore Foodo for Sayur Lodeh. Values consistent with known vegetable-coconut curry composition. Low sodium values consistent with a belacan-optional preparation.
notes: Sayur Lodeh's nutritional profile varies significantly based on (1) coconut milk concentration (thick vs. thin) and (2) whether belacan is used. The HPB Singapore values reflect a moderate coconut milk version. Vitamin A (62mcg RAE/100g) from carrot in the dish — a 300g serving provides 186mcg RAE (21% of the 900mcg/day RDA for men). Cholesterol-free (no animal fat beyond any trace from coconut milk — plant-based). Frequently paired with lontong (#152) as the 'two-component' Malay breakfast dish.
